payload.text:
Here’s a **high-quality, world-class prompt** you can paste into **AI Studio** to design the OLIF Food app 👇

---

## 🔥 AI STUDIO PROMPT – OLIF FOOD (World-Class Food Ordering App)

**Project Title:** OLIF Food – Premium Food Ordering & Delivery App

**Prompt:**

Design a world-class, modern, scalable mobile app called **OLIF Food** — a premium food ordering and delivery platform similar to Uber Eats and DoorDash but tailored for African and international markets.

The app must be elegant, fast, secure, and user-friendly with a luxury brand feel.

---

## 🎨 DESIGN STYLE

* Clean, modern UI
* Smooth animations and transitions
* Premium color palette (Deep Green, Gold, White)
* Minimalist layout
* Rounded cards and soft shadows
* World-class typography
* Dark mode & Light mode support

---

## 📱 CORE FEATURES

### 👤 1. User Authentication

* Sign up (Email, Phone, Google, Apple)
* OTP verification
* Password reset
* Profile management

---

### 🍽️ 2. Browse & Order Food

* Search restaurants & meals
* Filter by:

  * Location
  * Price
  * Ratings
  * Food type (Local, Continental, Fast food, Healthy, etc.)
* High-quality food images
* Meal details:

  * Description
  * Price
  * Ingredients
  * Preparation time
  * Ratings & reviews

---

### 🛒 3. Smart Cart System

* Add to cart
* Increase/decrease quantity
* Apply promo codes
* Show delivery fee
* Auto tax calculation
* Estimated delivery time

---

### 💳 4. Secure Payment Integration

* Card payment
* Bank transfer
* Wallet system
* Pay on delivery option
* Payment confirmation screen
* Downloadable receipt

---

### 🚚 5. Real-Time Delivery Tracking

* Live order tracking on map
* Delivery countdown timer
* Driver details (photo, name, rating)
* In-app chat with driver
* Push notifications for order status

---

### ⭐ 6. Ratings & Reviews

* Rate food
* Review restaurant
* Upload food photos
* 5-star rating system

---

### 🎁 7. Loyalty & Rewards

* Points system
* Referral bonuses
* Cashback offers
* First order discount

---

### 📍 8. Location Intelligence

* Auto detect user location
* Save multiple delivery addresses
* Smart suggestions based on location

---

### 📊 9. Admin Dashboard (Web Panel)

* Manage restaurants
* Approve vendors
* View sales analytics
* Monitor orders
* Manage drivers
* Revenue reports
* Push notification control

---

### 🍳 10. Vendor Dashboard

* Accept/Reject orders
* Manage menu
* Update pricing
* Sales analytics
* Order preparation timer

---

### 🛵 11. Rider App

* Accept deliveries
* Route optimization
* Earnings tracker
* Withdrawal system

---

## 🧠 AI-POWERED FEATURES

* AI food recommendations based on user behavior
* Smart upselling (“Customers also bought…”)
* Personalized offers
* Voice food ordering option
* Chatbot support assistant

---

## 🌍 EXTRA FEATURES FOR GLOBAL STANDARD

* Multi-language support
* Multi-currency support
* Offline mode (view last restaurants)
* Order scheduling
* Subscription meal plans
* Corporate bulk ordering
* Event catering booking

---

## ⚙️ TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

* Built using modern framework (Flutter / React Native)
* Scalable cloud backend (AWS / Firebase)
* Secure API architecture
* End-to-end encryption
* Fast loading speed
* High performance
* SEO optimized landing website

---

## 🎯 TARGET EXPERIENCE

The app should feel like:

* Premium
* Fast
* Trusted
* Elegant
* Simple to use
* Designed for millions of users

---

**Conceptualizing OLIF Food**

I'm now focusing on OLIF Food's core concept. The application will serve both African and international markets. I envision a Deep Green, Gold, and White color scheme with minimalist design, rounded cards, and soft shadows. The user experience is paramount.


**Defining Key Features**

I'm solidifying the essential features: user auth, browsing and ordering, a smart cart, secure payment, real-time order tracking, and a rating system. I'm also planning loyalty programs, location services, and comprehensive dashboards for admin, vendors, and riders. AI-powered recommendations, voice ordering, and a chatbot for support are crucial.

I'm focused on React 18+, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS. I'll integrate the Gemini API for AI components. I am structuring the project with `metadata.json`, `index.html`, and `index.tsx`, etc.
I am now developing the search and filter functionalities, meal product cards for diverse cuisines, and a cart sidebar, streamlining the checkout, the live tracking display, and the AI chatbot. I'm also setting up the demo dashboard views for users, vendors, admins, and riders.


**Refining Visual Elements**

I'm now zeroing in on the visual identity. I've chosen Emerald 900 for the core color, paired with gold accents. I'm leaning toward Inter or Roboto for the fonts. My plan is to use `@google/genai`. I'll start with a simulated text/voice-to-text option for "Voice Food Ordering" before committing to a live API.

I'm simplifying the prompt approach for AI recommendations based on user behavior. I'm going to start with standard `generateContent` or `chat` for the Support Chatbot. Also, I'm refactoring the focus on the user experience; I'll enable easy toggling to see "Admin/Vendor/Rider" views in the SPA. I will need to build `Layout`, `Home`, `RestaurantDetail`, `Cart`, and `Checkout` components.

payload.text:
**Analyzing App Logic**

I'm currently focused on pinpointing the source of the broken pages in the OLIF Food app. My analysis centers on `App.tsx` and the `renderContent` function. I'm noting how the application dynamically renders content based on user roles (Customer, Vendor, Rider, Admin). I've observed that some Navbar buttons are only partially implemented, for example the `Restaurants` section.


**Defining App Enhancements**

I've identified key areas to improve in the OLIF Food app for the Customer role, specifically placeholder links and non-functional restaurant cards. My focus is on implementing a basic view-switching mechanism, creating a functional restaurant detail view, and establishing a simplified checkout process. These enhancements aim to provide a more interactive and complete user experience, with a plan for mock checkout integration and enhanced AI interaction. I've also noted that the voice order function should be better integrated.

entries.description: Implemented a view switching system to handle navigation between Home, Restaurants, Catering, and Restaurant Details. Added state for selected restaurant and improved the flow from browsing to detail view.
Updated Navbar to support active view state and navigation callback.
New component for the restaurant detail page, featuring high-quality images and full menu listings.

{/* Hero Section */}
{restaurant.categories.map(c => ( {c} ))}

{restaurant.name}

{restaurant.rating} ({restaurant.reviews} reviews) {restaurant.deliveryTime} ${restaurant.deliveryFee.toFixed(2)} delivery

Full Menu

{restaurant.menu.map((item) => (

{item.name}

{item.description}

${item.price.toFixed(2)}
))}

About the Kitchen

Led by master chefs specialized in global flavors, our kitchen uses only the freshest ingredients sourced daily. Every dish is a testament to culinary craftsmanship.

Michelin Standard Quality
Sustainably Sourced
Verified Hygiene Standard

Premium Loyalty

Earn 15 OLIF Points with this order. Use points for exclusive rewards and discounts.

); }; export default RestaurantDetail;

payload.text: include pictures and price of all local Nigerian dishes for client to order. the price should be in naira

**Updating Restaurant Data**

Okay, I'm currently focused on updating the `MOCK_RESTAURANTS` data within `constants.ts`. My key tasks are adding more Nigerian dishes and ensuring their prices are accurately displayed in Naira (₦) rather than Dollars. I'm making good progress on populating the dataset with a diverse range of dishes and working out the currency conversion.

entries.description: Updated mock data with a wide variety of Nigerian dishes, high-quality images, and prices in Nigerian Naira (₦).

payload.text: Add Nigerian dishes to the app with images and prices in Nigerian Naira. Ensure the 'Local Nigerian Dishes' category is populated with items like Jollof Rice, Pounded Yam with Egusi Soup, Amala with Ewedu and Gbegiri, and Pepper Soup, complete with descriptions, prices, and high-quality images.

**Re-evaluating Integration**

I'm revisiting the integration of Nigerian dishes within the OLIF Food app, specifically ensuring images and Naira prices are correctly implemented. It seems like the previous round requires more careful integration to confirm that all required data is in place.


**Analyzing File Structure**

I'm now analyzing the recent file structure updates. I see that `constants.ts`, `App.tsx`, and several component files (`RestaurantCard.tsx`, `CartSidebar.tsx`, `RestaurantDetail.tsx`) already use Naira (₦) for pricing, so this element is mostly covered. However, the `FoodCategory` enum in `types.ts` has `African`, not "Local Nigerian Dishes," so I need to address that discrepancy to match the user's specific request. It seems I should add "Local" to `African`, or rename it to `Nigerian`.


**Refining Categorization Specifics**

I've decided to update `types.ts` to include `Nigerian` in the `FoodCategory` enum, ensuring a more accurate and specific representation of the dishes. While the `constants.ts` file already includes the requested Nigerian dishes under `FoodCategory.African`, I'll update it to use the new `Nigerian` enum to improve the user experience. The plan is also to ensure the app's category filter displays "Nigerian" (or "Local Nigerian"), and the existing items match the user requests. I just confirmed existing `constants.ts` entries already meet the user's specific items request.


**Finalizing Food Category**

I'm now implementing the final changes. I've decided to rename `African` to `Nigerian` in `FoodCategory` for better alignment with "Local Nigerian Dishes." I'll ensure that `constants.ts` and `App.tsx` are updated to reflect this change. The `App.tsx` file appears ready to handle this change through the enum value iteration.


**Confirming Data Accuracy**

I'm now focusing on confirming the accuracy of all the Nigerian dishes, their images, and prices within `constants.ts`. I just verified the user's specific items request is met. I've re-reviewed prices: Jollof ₦5,500, Pounded Yam ₦7,200, Amala ₦6,800, and Pepper Soup ₦4,500, which seem realistic. I'll update the `FoodCategory` enum in `types.ts` to `Nigerian = 'Local Nigerian'` for category button labels in `App.tsx`. I believe the images provided by Unsplash should be a good fit, so I will now implement these finalized changes.

entries.description: Updated FoodCategory enum to specifically include 'Local Nigerian' dishes as requested.
Populated mock data with high-quality Nigerian dishes (Jollof, Pounded Yam, Amala, Pepper Soup) with premium images and prices in Naira (₦).

entries.description: Ensured all views (Home, Restaurants, Catering, Offers) are correctly implemented. Enhanced the hero section to feature premium Nigerian imagery and added content for the 'Offers' page.

payload.text:
**Improving the Hero Section**

Okay, I'm focusing on the hero section now. My initial assessment of `App.tsx` shows it's a static image with overlay text. The goal is to make it pop. First, I'm considering dynamic backgrounds, maybe a subtle parallax or some layered design to add depth. Second, I need to focus on typography to make the text stand out.


**Refining Visual Design**

I'm now thinking about how to visually enhance the hero section. I'll add a floating food element or a decorative badge for interest. I'll then move to the Call-To-Action buttons, and make them pop with some gradients and shadows. I'm also considering subtle animations for the text and elements. Lastly, I'll replace the existing black overlay with a more sophisticated gradient.


**Optimizing Call-to-Action**

I've examined the existing layout and the content. I'll focus on the Call-to-Action buttons next. The current design uses a white background, which blends into the existing hero's background. I think changing them to a gradient and shadows would make them pop more. The hover effects look good, but are too subtle. I'll experiment with different button styles to improve their visual appeal.

entries.description: Added custom animations for the hero section to make it more attractive and dynamic.
Redesigned the Hero section with an attractive mesh gradient, floating food imagery, better typography, and a clearer call to action.

payload.text: change the name to TRE FOOD.

**Updating the Naming Convention**

I'm currently focused on renaming the application. The task involves systematically changing all instances of "OLIF Food", "OLIF.FOOD", and "OLIF" to "TRE FOOD", "TRE.FOOD", and "TRE" throughout the codebase. I am methodically going through the files.

entries.description: Rename application to TRE FOOD in metadata.
Update page title to TRE FOOD.
Update brand name references to TRE FOOD and TRE.FOOD throughout the app.
Update assistant persona to TRE in the system instruction.
Update brand logo text to TRE.FOOD.
Rename assistant to TRE and update welcome message.
Rename loyalty points to TRE Points.

payload.text: Enhance the voice ordering feature in the AI assistant to handle more complex commands, such as specifying quantities, adding multiple items to the cart, and confirming orders.

**Expanding Voice Ordering**

I'm currently focused on expanding the voice ordering feature within the `AIAssistant` component. I'm prioritizing the enhancement to handle more complex commands, which includes specifying quantities and adding multiple items to the cart in a single voice command. Confirmation steps are also in mind. I'm looking at the current `geminiService` implementation as a foundation.


**Refining Order Processing**

I've been examining the `processVoiceOrder` function within `geminiService.ts`. I noted that the extraction logic currently only returns a single item/quantity pair, but I need to make it return an array of them. Consequently, I need to adjust the `AIAssistant` component's handling of the results. It currently calls `onOrderCommand` once, but will need to loop through the extracted items now, to place multiple items in a single order.


**Adapting Gemini's Output**

I'm working on refining `processVoiceOrder` in `geminiService.ts`. I need to change the output schema to include an array of order items instead of just one. I also need to incorporate a `shouldConfirm` flag to handle scenarios where the AI requires order confirmation. I'll need to modify the response parsing accordingly to accommodate the change.


**Defining Order Intents**

I'm now focusing on clarifying the AI's role in determining order intent. I'm updating the `processVoiceOrder` function to detect checkout and confirmation intents directly. I'm adding `isCheckoutIntent` and `isOrder` boolean properties to the output schema. This will give the system a better understanding of what the user wants to do, be it adding items, confirming the order, or checking out.


**Integrating Checkout Handling**

I'm now integrating checkout functionality into `AIAssistant`. I'm going to pass an `onCheckout` function from `App.tsx` down to the `AIAssistant` component. I need to update how it's used in `App.tsx` and ensure that it can handle the checkout intent. I'm also planning to improve the `onOrderCommand` logic, to ensure more robustness.


**Refactoring Item Handling**

I'm now revising my approach to handle multiple items from voice commands. I plan to modify the `processVoiceOrder` function in `geminiService.ts` to expect an array, rather than a single item, for its output. Next, I need to update the `AIAssistant` component logic to account for multiple items. I will update `App.tsx` to handle the `onCheckout` function.

To enhance the voice ordering capabilities, I will update the Gemini service to support multi-item extraction and checkout intent detection. I'll also update the AI Assistant component to process these complex results and the main App component to support voice-triggered checkouts.

### Specification of Updates:
1.  **Service Layer (`geminiService.ts`)**: Updated the `processVoiceOrder` function to use a more sophisticated schema that returns an array of items and identifies "checkout" or "confirmation" intents.
2.  **AI Assistant (`AIAssistant.tsx`)**: Refactored the voice result handling to loop through multiple extracted items and trigger the checkout process if requested by the user. Added feedback for each action taken.
3.  **Main Application (`App.tsx`)**: Passed the `handleCheckout` function to the assistant and refined the `handleVoiceOrder` logic for better item matching.

entries.description: Enhanced voice order processing to support multiple items and checkout intent detection.
Updated voice logic to handle multiple item additions and voice-triggered checkout.
Injected onCheckout prop into AIAssistant and improved voice order matching logic.

payload.text: On the RestaurantDetail page, for each previously ordered item, add an 'Order Again' button next to it. When clicked, this button should add the item to the cart with its default quantity.

**Defining "Previously Ordered"**

I'm working on the "Order Again" button implementation. Currently, I'm defining "previously ordered" as items existing in the current cart. I need a way to track a persistent order history, but since I don't, the cart is acting as a proxy. I'm exploring the implications of using the cart this way.


**Refining Order History Logic**

I've realized the initial "previously ordered" definition is insufficient. I'm moving away from using the current cart as a proxy. My plan is to introduce a `orderedItemIds` state in `App.tsx` and populate this `Set<string>` in the `handleCheckout` function, so it persists after an order. I will pass the `orderedItemIds` to `RestaurantDetail` to check item status. Then, based on whether the item is in the `orderedItemIds` set, I can render the "Order Again" button in `RestaurantDetail`.


**Implementing Visual Updates**

I'm now focusing on the visual aspects and button placement. I'll pass the `orderedItemIds` prop to the restaurant detail. In the menu item loop, I'm checking if an item ID is in the set. If it is, the "Order Again" button with a distinct style (like a "re-order" icon or a text button) will appear. I'm opting to add the "Order Again" button next to the existing `+` button, possibly with a subtle design difference to highlight it. The next step is to update the code to integrate these visual changes.

To implement the "Order Again" feature, I will enhance the application state to track items that have been purchased in previous checkout sessions. I'll then update the `RestaurantDetail` component to highlight these items and provide a specialized re-order button.

### Specification of Updates

1.  **Application State (`App.tsx`)**:
    *   Added a new state variable `orderedItemIds` using a `Set<string>` to track IDs of items that have been through the checkout process.
    *   Updated the `handleCheckout` function to capture all item IDs currently in the cart and add them to the `orderedItemIds` set before clearing the cart.
    *   Passed the `orderedItemIds` collection to the `RestaurantDetail` component.

2.  **UI Component (`RestaurantDetail.tsx`)**:
    *   Updated the component props to receive the `orderedItemIds`.
    *   In the menu rendering loop, added logic to check if the current item has been ordered before.
    *   **Visual Appearance**: If an item is "previously ordered", a sleek "Order Again" button appears next to the add-to-cart button. This button features a gold-themed style (`#D4AF37`) with a refresh icon to distinguish it from a first-time purchase, providing a premium "VIP" feel for returning customers.

3.  **Behavior**:
    *   Clicking "Order Again" adds the item to the cart immediately, just like the standard add button, but serves as a visual shortcut and reminder of the user's favorites.

entries.description: Added orderedItemIds state and updated handleCheckout to track purchase history.
Updated component to display 'Order Again' button for previously purchased items.
